name: Cognitive Narratology (Fludernik, Herman)
core_claim: Cognitive narratology claims that narrative is not primarily a structure of plot elements but a cognitive ACTIVITY of the mind — the central thing that drives engagement is the reader/viewer's active mental construction of, and immersion in, a 'storyworld' populated by experiencing consciousnesses. Two foundational moves anchor the field. Monika Fludernik (Towards a 'Natural' Narratology, 1996) relocates the defining property of narrative — 'narrativity' — away from plot and event sequence and onto EXPERIENTIALITY: the quasi-mimetic evocation of what it is like to be a consciousness undergoing real-life experience. On her account a text is narrative to the degree it conveys embodied, perspectival, emotionally-laden human (or anthropomorphic) experience grounded in everyday cognitive schemata, not to the degree it has a tidy beginning-middle-end. David Herman (Story Logic, 2002; Storytelling and the Sciences of Mind, 2013) reframes narrative comprehension as STORYWORLD construction: readers use textual cues as a 'blueprint' to mentally model a world — its space, time, participants, and states/events — and then inhabit it via a 'deictic shift' into the WHO/WHEN/WHERE of the story. Engagement, on this view, is the work of building, updating, and dwelling inside that simulated world. Two further claims complete the picture: (a) EMBODIED SIMULATION — comprehension recruits the reader's own sensorimotor and emotional systems to enact the storyworld, so understanding a narrated action partly re-enacts it; and (b) GAP-FILLING — drawing on Iser's reader-response work and on schema/frame theory, the text is radically incomplete and the reader's pleasure and labour lie in supplying the missing connections (blanks, inferences, default knowledge). So the driver of engagement is the richness and tractability of the cognitive work the story affords: a vivid, inhabitable, experientially dense world that gives the mind productive simulation and inference to do.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Squarely EXPERIENTIAL, but at a deeper and more process-oriented level than affect-valence taxonomies — it is a theory of the COGNITIVE PROCESS by which any story becomes experienceable at all. It is not about what a story is topically about (descriptive), nor only about how it feels emotionally (affective valence), but about the mode and quality of MIND-WORK the story induces: world-modelling, perspective-taking, embodied re-enactment, and inferential gap-filling. It therefore occupies a constructive/processual dimension. It is adjacent to but distinct from narrative transportation theory: transportation measures the intensity of the absorptive STATE as an outcome; cognitive narratology specifies the underlying MECHANISMS (storyworld construction, deictic shift, simulation, gap-filling) that produce that state and explains WHICH textual features feed them. Fludernik's 'experientiality' in particular is a definitional criterion of narrativity, not a content label — it says a character-bound, consciousness-centred, embodied texture is what makes something feel like a story rather than a report.
key_dimensions: The framework offers a cluster of process axes / constructs rather than a closed list of categories: (1) EXPERIENTIALITY (Fludernik) — the degree to which the work foregrounds an embodied, perspectival, emotion-laden consciousness undergoing experience, vs. impersonal report; her cognitively-grounded prototype frames are TELLING, EXPERIENCING, VIEWING, ACTING, and REFLECTING (the 'natural' macro-frames narrative draws on). (2) STORYWORLD richness and inhabitability (Herman) — how fully the text specifies the WHO (participants/agents), WHAT (states, events, actions), WHEN (temporal structure), WHERE (spatial layout), and WHY (causal/intentional logic) of a world the reader can model. (3) DEICTIC SHIFT / deictic centre — the ease and pull of relocating one's here-now-I into the story's coordinates (perspective and immersion). (4) EMBODIED SIMULATION — the extent to which the narrated content recruits sensorimotor, perceptual, and affective re-enactment (concrete action, sensory detail, bodily emotion). (5) GAP-FILLING / inferential demand — the quantity and type of 'blanks' (Iser), default-knowledge frames/scripts (Schank & Abelson; Emmott's contextual frames), and indeterminacies the reader must actively close; an engagement 'sweet spot' between too-explicit (no work) and too-gappy (incoherent). (6) MIND-MODELLING / Theory-of-Mind load (Zunshine, Palmer's 'fictional minds') — the demand to attribute beliefs, intentions, and nested mental states to characters. (7) FOCALISATION / consciousness-representation — whose mind we are inside and how directly (free indirect discourse, interior monologue). Related operational construct: Storyworld Possible Selves (Martinez) — the reader's projection of self-versions into the storyworld, with empirical (Check-All-That-Apply) measurement.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed and only partly tested. The field's strongest universality claim — that human minds comprehend narrative by building experiential storyworlds via embodied cognition and TOM — is plausibly pan-human and rests on cognitive-science foundations (embodied cognition, schema theory, mentalising) that are broadly cross-cultural. Fludernik's 'natural' narratology explicitly grounds itself in oral, everyday conversational storytelling (drawing on Labov's sociolinguistics), which gives it some claim to a cross-culturally common substrate. HOWEVER the framework is implicitly Western/Eurocentric in important ways: (1) its corpus and exemplars are overwhelmingly English/European literary fiction across historical periods; (2) the specific SCHEMAS and FRAMES that drive gap-filling are culturally learned default knowledge — what counts as an obvious inference, a 'natural' script (restaurant, courtship, funeral), or an unmarked perspective varies by culture, so the SAME synopsis will license different gap-filling across audiences; (3) Theory-of-Mind / mind-attribution norms and the convention of deep interiority (free indirect style) are historically and culturally specific literary developments, not universals. What breaks down cross-culturally is therefore not the mechanism but its CONTENT: the schemas, the salience of interiority, and the default inferences. Systematic cross-cultural empirical validation of the specific constructs is thin. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Mixed quality, improving. The CORE theory is humanistic/conceptual — Fludernik's and Herman's books are works of literary theory and close reading, argued by analysis of texts rather than tested against enjoyment or completion data. That said, the framework has spawned and connected to a substantial EMPIRICAL programme: (a) embodied-simulation reading research in cognitive psychology and neuroscience (e.g. sensorimotor and language-region activation during action sentences; mental-imagery studies) provides experimental support for the simulation claim; (b) empirical reader-response / 'scientific study of literature' work (IGEL tradition, journal Scientific Study of Literature) tests gap-filling, foregrounding, and immersion with flesh-and-blood readers; (c) the Storyworld Possible Selves construct has been operationalised and tested with Check-All-That-Apply consumer-research methods and immersion measures (Martinez and colleagues, 2014-2025). Evidence type spans experimental (simulation, foregrounding), correlational/survey (immersion, SPS), and predominantly interpretive/anecdotal for the literary-theory core. There is NO large catalogue-scale validation tying these constructs to actual completion or preference data at recommender scale. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework you would need: a corpus annotated for experientiality / storyworld-specification / inferential-demand, with matched per-viewer enjoyment or completion outcomes, then show the synopsis-derived proxies predict outcomes — and crucially, model gap-filling as viewer-relative since required inferences depend on each viewer's prior schemas.
operationalisability: Partially extractable, with one cleanly extractable axis and several that are only coarsely or relationally estimable. EXTRACTABLE from a synopsis (work-side, whole-work or arc unit): a rough EXPERIENTIALITY signal (does the description centre a consciousness undergoing felt experience — 'a grieving widow', 'a soldier's inner unravelling' — vs. an impersonal premise?); STORYWORLD specificity (how concretely the world's where/when/who are named and textured); presence of deep-interiority signals (character-study, stream-of-consciousness, memoir framing); and MIND-MODELLING / ToM load (ensemble of agents with conflicting intentions, deception, nested motives). PARTIALLY/UNRELIABLY extractable: GAP-FILLING demand — a synopsis cannot reveal how gappy the actual text/film is, because synopses normalise and flatten ambiguity; markers like 'ambiguous ending', 'unreliable narrator', 'nonlinear' are weak proxies. NOT extractable from synopsis: embodied-simulation intensity at the line/shot level (a craft property of prose/direction, invisible in summary), and — critically — the RELATIONAL gap-filling load, since the inferences a story demands depend on the specific viewer's prior schemas. Natural extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK for an experientiality/interiority prior, with arc-level estimation for serialised TV. Failure modes: (1) mistaking plot-summary density for storyworld richness (a dense plot can be experientially thin); (2) treating gap-filling/ambiguity as a work property when it is reader-relative; (3) marketing copy that asserts immersion regardless of craft; (4) flattening focalisation — synopses rarely say whose mind we inhabit; (5) conflating high information with high experientiality.
motivation_served: Maps to a blend, but its centre of gravity is the COGNITIVE-AESTHETIC pleasure of world-inhabitation and meaning-construction — closer to EUDAIMONIC appreciation (being moved, perspective-taking, dwelling in another consciousness) while also feeding HEDONIC immersion/escapism (a richly inhabitable storyworld is pleasurable to be in) and MOOD REGULATION (escape into a simulated world). The gap-filling dimension adds a distinctly cognitive 'pleasure of the puzzle / productive inference' motive, and the mind-modelling dimension serves a social-cognitive / empathy need (training Theory of Mind). For GROUP viewing: two viewers must be able to inhabit and successfully build the SAME storyworld together. The framework predicts friction when partners differ in the schemas needed to fill the story's gaps (one finds an elliptical art film richly inferable, the other finds it incoherent) or in tolerance for deep interiority vs. external action. A safe joint pick maximises broadly inhabitable storyworlds and inference demands within both viewers' shared schema repertoire — accessible world specification and moderate, not idiosyncratic, gap-filling.
receiver_dependence: Strongly MIXED, and this is the framework's sharpest contribution for a two-person recommender. Some constructs are largely WORK-side and absolute: a work either does or does not specify a richly textured storyworld; it either centres a felt consciousness (high experientiality) or reads as impersonal report; it either represents deep interiority or stays external. These are comparatively stable properties of the work. But the two engine constructs — GAP-FILLING and SCHEMA-BASED INFERENCE — are intrinsically RELATIONAL: the gaps a text leaves can only be filled with the reader's own prior knowledge, frames, and scripts, so the very same elliptical story is rich and inferable for one viewer and confusing or inert for another. Embodied simulation is likewise person-relative (it recruits the individual's own sensorimotor/affective repertoire), and Theory-of-Mind load lands differently by viewer mentalising capacity and cultural mind-norms. Storyworld Possible Selves is explicitly relational — the self a viewer projects into the world depends on that viewer. For HelpME2C this means a cognitive-narratology axis must be split: store work-side experientiality / interiority / storyworld-richness as catalogue tags, but model inferential-demand and schema-fit as a relational layer that depends on each user's history and prior knowledge — never collapse the gap-filling load into a single work property.
blind_spots: (1) PLOT-light bias: by relocating narrativity onto experientiality, Fludernik's framework under-values strongly plot-driven, low-interiority pleasures (procedurals, heist mechanics, twist-thrillers) that many viewers love precisely for event-logic, not felt consciousness. (2) It says little about WHICH content a given person wants — like transportation theory, it theorises the MODE of comprehension, not thematic preference, so on its own it cannot drive topical recommendation. (3) GENRE/spectacle blindness: it is built on literary fiction and oral narrative, and under-theorises non-experiential cinematic pleasures (action spectacle, comedy timing, visual style, music) that are central to TV/film enjoyment but barely textual. (4) Craft invisibility: its key engine — embodied simulation and gap-filling at the sentence/shot level — is a property of execution invisible in synopses, limiting computational use. (5) It can over-intellectualise enjoyment, neglecting comfort re-watching, ambient viewing, and pure affective spectacle where little active world-building occurs. (6) Weak on SOCIAL/ritual co-viewing, which matters for the group case. (7) Its gap-filling claims are hard to falsify and the empirical literary-theory core is largely interpretive. (8) Cross-culturally, it treats the reader's schema repertoire as a given rather than a major source of variance.
vocabulary_implications: Cognitive narratology is NOT a set of themes; like transportation theory it contributes a cross-cutting EXPERIENTIAL/PROCESS layer that should sit beside the topical theme vocabulary, not inside it. Proposed work-side, synopsis-estimable slugs/scores: 'high-interiority' (story centred on a consciousness's felt inner experience — distinct from plot-driven), 'rich-storyworld' (densely specified, inhabitable world — distinct from 'immersive-world' which is setting flavour; this is world-modelling tractability), 'mind-game / inference-demanding' (high gap-filling and ambiguity — but flag as only weakly synopsis-detectable and partly relational), 'multi-perspective-minds' (high Theory-of-Mind / nested-intention load), and reuse the existing 'character-study' slug as the canonical high-experientiality, deep-focalisation marker. A coarse ordinal 'experientiality-level' (low/medium/high) could be estimated from synopsis as a work-side prior. Flag explicitly as NOT extractable from synopsis and requiring other data sources: actual gap-filling/inferential LOAD relative to a viewer (needs per-user schema/history modelling, not catalogue text), embodied-simulation intensity (a craft property needing reviews/critical signals), and Storyworld Possible Selves fit (intrinsically per-user). Practical encoding: keep experientiality / interiority / storyworld-richness as LLM-estimated work-side tags, but route inference-demand and schema-fit into a separate relational user×work layer; surfacing 'inference-demanding' as a flat work tag would mislabel large relational variance.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Operates one level up from the topical/moral/identity slugs as an experiential-process facet, and overlaps with the texture group. It VALIDATES texture slugs that are really mode-of-engagement descriptors: 'character-study' (the cleanest existing proxy for high experientiality / deep focalisation), 'meditation-on-time' / 'meditation-on-death' (reflective, consciousness-centred experientiality), 'nonlinear-storytelling' (raises gap-filling/inferential demand), 'magical-realism' (storyworld-construction strain that demands active schema accommodation), and 'slow-burn' (delayed-payoff experiential dwelling). It strongly suggests these texture slugs are MISALIGNED filed alongside topical themes — they are experiential-axis descriptors and would be cleaner as a distinct facet, the same conclusion narrative-transportation research reaches. GAPS it reveals: HelpME2C has no explicit handle on (a) EXPERIENTIALITY / interiority vs. plot-drivenness — a major axis distinguishing two works that share every theme slug; (b) STORYWORLD richness/inhabitability as a tractability property distinct from setting; (c) inferential / gap-filling DEMAND (and no mechanism for its relational nature); (d) Theory-of-Mind / mind-modelling load. It also reinforces, from a different theoretical base than transportation theory, that the vocabulary lacks any layer for receiver-dependent variance — here specifically schema-driven gap-filling, which the framework shows is large and viewer-specific.
key_sources: Fludernik, M. (1996). Towards a 'Natural' Narratology. Routledge (foundational; introduces experientiality and the natural macro-frames; paywalled/print, overview on Google Books: https://books.google.com/books/about/Towards_a_Natural_Narratology.html?id=p_WHAgAAQBAJ). Herman, D. (2002). Story Logic: Problems and Possibilities of Narrative. Univ. of Nebraska Press (storyworld, story logic; print/paywalled). Herman, D. (2013). Storytelling and the Sciences of Mind. MIT Press (embodied/enactivist cognitive narratology; paywalled). Fludernik, M. & Alber et al., 'Experientiality' entry, the living handbook of narratology — FREE: https://www-archiv.fdm.uni-hamburg.de/lhn/node/102.html. Iser, W. (1978). The Act of Reading (gap-filling / blanks; print). Zunshine, L. (2006). Why We Read Fiction: Theory of Mind and the Novel (mind-modelling; print). Martinez, M.-A. (2014/2018). Storyworld Possible Selves — open PDFs via ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/323656471_Storyworld_Possible_Selves and https://www.researchgate.net/publication/270337435. 'Imagining emotions in storyworlds' (2024), open access PMC: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10848170/. Check-All-That-Apply / SPS empirical (2025), Sage: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/02762374251360128 (paywalled). Cognitive-narratology overview blog (free): https://diegesis-in-mind.com/2022/10/01/how-i-became-a-cognitive-narratologist-fluderniks-towards-a-natural-narratology/.
